rules.expected_behavior: Surface mismatch and classify according to documented exception status.
rules.violation_behavior: Emit mismatch note or fail consistency if not documented.
rules.remediation_guidance: Align readiness summary with executable state or mark as preserved sentinel.

trigger_conditions: provider missing or invalid while binding claims validated
rules.expected_behavior: Binding must not validate without a valid provider chain.
rules.remediation_guidance: Invalidate binding or restore valid provider chain.

trigger_conditions: trust_state contains trust_blocked and final readiness is not blocked
rules.expected_behavior: Any trust block forces blocked readiness.
rules.violation_behavior: Fail consistency and mark terminal violation.
rules.remediation_guidance: Set final readiness to blocked whenever trust is blocked.

trigger_conditions: approval_state is approval_expired while chain remains execution-ready
rules.expected_behavior: Expired approvals must block execution readiness.
rules.remediation_guidance: Require a fresh approval before any execution-ready classification.

trigger_conditions: rollback invalid for risky action while chain remains execution-ready
rules.expected_behavior: Rollback invalidity blocks risky execution readiness.
rules.remediation_guidance: Provide a valid rollback policy before restoring readiness.

rules.rule_name: Blocked Outputs Must Emit Blocked Reasons
trigger_conditions: final readiness blocked but blocked reason tokens/objects absent
rules.expected_behavior: Blocked outputs emit stable blocked-reason tokens and objects.
rules.remediation_guidance: Emit matching blocked reason tokens and rich blocked reason objects.

rules.expected_behavior: Tenant violations force blocked readiness regardless of approval presence.
rules.violation_behavior: Fail consistency and terminally block chain.
rules.remediation_guidance: Restore tenant-compliant roots/apps/toolpacks before further validation.

rules.expected_behavior: No execution-ready state may survive trust-blocked posture.
rules.violation_behavior: Fail consistency and force blocked readiness.
rules.remediation_guidance: Resolve trust assignment before any higher readiness class is allowed.

trigger_conditions: provider missing while approval state is present or required
rules.expected_behavior: Provider absence blocks chain before approval can matter.
rules.violation_behavior: Fail expectation or consistency if approval is treated as sufficient.
rules.remediation_guidance: Restore provider chain before interpreting approval state.

trigger_conditions: approval present while provider invalid or missing
rules.expected_behavior: Approval presence must not imply execution readiness without valid provider chain.
rules.violation_behavior: Fail consistency if approval is treated as sufficient.
rules.remediation_guidance: Re-establish provider validity before approval can have effect.

narratives.technical_summary: Dependency references are complete, cross-linked, and internally consistent for static governance interpretation. Maturity, completeness, escalation, and evidence surfaces align without creating runtime authority.
narratives.governance_summary: The packet may be treated as strongly prepared for static review and communication, but not as approval for implementation, execution, or release.
narratives.remediation_expectation: Only routine maintenance and reference refreshes are expected unless a dependent artifact materially changes.
narratives.escalation_expectation: No immediate escalation is required beyond ordinary review cadence; retain security gate awareness where applicable.
narratives.prohibited_interpretation: Do not interpret excellent static health as permission to execute tools, run bridges, or publish releases.

narratives.technical_summary: Core dependency artifacts are present and credible, with only minor narrative or packaging gaps that do not compromise static governance usefulness.
narratives.governance_summary: The packet is fit for continued static review and audience-ready packaging, but active security and authorization constraints still govern next-step limits.
narratives.remediation_expectation: Address minor evidence, wording, or cross-reference gaps during the next maintenance pass.
narratives.escalation_expectation: Escalate only if a minor gap begins to create interpretive risk or signoff ambiguity.
narratives.prohibited_interpretation: Do not collapse strong static health into implementation-candidate approval or release readiness.

narratives.technical_summary: Dependencies are mostly present, but at least one supporting reference, signoff surface, or crosswalk narrative needs review before the packet should be treated as stable for its target audience.
narratives.governance_summary: The packet can continue in the static lane, but reviewers should treat it as provisional until the identified review items are resolved.
narratives.remediation_expectation: Refresh the missing or weak references, then rerun the applicable checklist pack before relying on the packet.
narratives.escalation_expectation: Escalate to governance ownership if unresolved review items begin to affect audience interpretation or readiness scoring.
narratives.prohibited_interpretation: Do not present a review-needed packet as finalized, approved, or implementation-capable.

narratives.technical_summary: One or more dependencies, signoff links, or evidence bundles are incomplete enough to reduce confidence in the packet’s static readiness posture.
narratives.governance_summary: The packet should remain in remediation until the missing references and blocked checklist items are corrected or explicitly waived.
narratives.remediation_expectation: Complete the missing artifacts, tighten the narrative language, and document the remediation outcome before re-review.
narratives.escalation_expectation: Escalate to the designated review or governance owner if remediation spans multiple dependent artifacts or delays review milestones.
narratives.prohibited_interpretation: Do not route a remediation-required packet into release-facing, execution-facing, or implementation-facing decisions.

narratives.technical_summary: A terminal blocker remains active in the dependency chain, such as unresolved Envoy v0.2.8 security triage or a required signoff state that prevents advancement regardless of overall documentation maturity.
narratives.governance_summary: Static packaging may continue for analysis and communication, but readiness advancement is blocked until the named blocker is resolved or formally superseded.
narratives.remediation_expectation: Maintain the static-only boundary, document the blocker clearly, and wait for the blocking dependency to be resolved through the proper governance path.
narratives.escalation_expectation: Keep the blocker escalated to the relevant security, release, or governance owner until it is resolved, waived, or superseded.
narratives.prohibited_interpretation: Do not treat blocked dependency health as a signal to bypass policy, reinterpret approval tokens, or proceed with release mutation.

scan_sensitive_surface_categories.reason_for_scanner_attention: Process-spawn surfaces often trigger heuristic concern because they can be used for command execution.
scan_sensitive_surface_categories.current_interpretation: scanner-sensitive but not by itself proof of malicious intent
scan_sensitive_surface_categories.documentation_action: Require bounded explanation, purpose description, and preserved non-execution review language.

scan_sensitive_surface_categories.surface_label: spawn or spawn-adjacent process launch behavior
scan_sensitive_surface_categories.reason_for_scanner_attention: Process launch behaviors may resemble automation or dropper patterns in broad heuristic scanners.
scan_sensitive_surface_categories.current_interpretation: requires contextual review rather than assumption of malware behavior
scan_sensitive_surface_categories.documentation_action: Separate legitimate orchestration rationale from unreviewed execution behavior.

scan_sensitive_surface_categories.surface_label: fs.writeFileSync or write-adjacent file mutation behavior
scan_sensitive_surface_categories.reason_for_scanner_attention: File-writing behavior can attract heuristic concern when combined with packaging or execution features.
scan_sensitive_surface_categories.current_interpretation: requires path-scope and intent explanation rather than automatic condemnation
scan_sensitive_surface_categories.documentation_action: Document bounded write intent and distinguish packaging/governance artifacts from release mutation.

scan_sensitive_surface_categories.surface_label: compiler bridge or runtime bridge orchestration references
scan_sensitive_surface_categories.reason_for_scanner_attention: Bridge/orchestration concepts can appear risky when read without operational boundaries.
scan_sensitive_surface_categories.current_interpretation: preserve as architecture work; do not infer unsafe behavior without evidence
scan_sensitive_surface_categories.documentation_action: Keep bridge-preservation and no-execution constraints explicit in all triage materials.

narratives.technical_detail: Maturity and completeness may support candidacy discussion, but active security triage still blocks implementation-facing movement.
narratives.remediation_or_next_step: Resolve or formally waive the active security blocker before reassessing candidacy.
narratives.prohibited_interpretation: Do not treat structural completeness as a security exception.

narratives.summary: Release readiness remains blocked by active Envoy v0.2.8 security triage.
narratives.technical_detail: No release mutation, publish step, or bridge-related alteration is permissible while the triage boundary remains active.
narratives.governance_note: Release posture remains blocked regardless of static governance completeness.
narratives.remediation_or_next_step: Maintain the no-release boundary until triage is resolved or formally waived by the appropriate owner.
narratives.prohibited_interpretation: Do not infer that a complete static governance pack permits release.

templates.compliance_summary: No execution authority was granted under the evaluated preview state.
templates.remediation_required: None unless execution authority was expected by policy.

templates.compliance_summary: Required approval was absent for the evaluated execution path.
templates.evidence_required: Approval-state records and expected-result references showing approval required.
templates.remediation_required: Obtain bounded approval according to policy before higher readiness is considered.

templates.compliance_summary: An approval token existed but was expired at evaluation time.
templates.evidence_required: Approval token metadata with granted_at and expires_at values.
templates.remediation_required: Issue a fresh bounded approval token if policy still allows the action.

templates.compliance_summary: Trust policy explicitly blocked the evaluated chain.
templates.evidence_required: Trust registry classification and corresponding blocked reason output.
templates.remediation_required: Review trust assignment; do not bypass the trust boundary.

templates.compliance_summary: Tenant boundary controls were violated by the evaluated chain.
templates.evidence_required: Tenant boundary definition plus blocked reason output and fixture trace references.
templates.remediation_required: Restore tenant-scoped roots, apps, and toolpack boundaries before retry.

templates.compliance_summary: Rollback posture was inadequate for the assessed risk or side-effect level.
templates.evidence_required: Rollback-state output and policy mapping for the affected chain.
templates.remediation_required: Adopt a compliant rollback model before execution-ready posture is reconsidered.

templates.compliance_summary: No valid provider existed for the declared capability chain.
templates.evidence_required: Provider-state output and binding/provider comparison records.
templates.remediation_required: Establish a governed provider chain before any higher readiness is considered.

templates.compliance_summary: A capability was declared without governed registry recognition.
templates.evidence_required: Capability-state output showing registry_missing or unknown capability posture.
templates.remediation_required: Register the capability or remove the undeclared capability reference.

templates.compliance_summary: Audit policy posture was insufficient for the evaluated chain.
templates.evidence_required: Audit-policy reference plus blocked reason output.
templates.remediation_required: Restore required audit policy before considering any higher readiness state.

templates.executive_summary_text: The evaluated chain is structurally valid for preview-only governance review and does not authorize execution.
templates.technical_summary_text: Capability, provider, and trust signals support preview semantics, while execution authority remains disabled by design.
templates.remediation_text: Continue with documentation or dry-run planning only if governance posture remains unchanged.
templates.audit_note_text: No execution was authorized or performed; preview-only posture was preserved.

templates.executive_summary_text: The chain is structurally suitable for dry-run interpretation but is not execution-ready.
templates.technical_summary_text: Provider and binding signals are present, but approval or mode constraints prevent higher readiness.
templates.remediation_text: Resolve approval or mode blockers before considering any future gated execution posture.
templates.audit_note_text: Dry-run-only state was maintained and no runtime authority was granted.

templates.executive_summary_text: The chain is structurally complete but remains subject to OpenClaw runtime gate approval.
templates.technical_summary_text: All structural checks pass, but execution authority is intentionally withheld until runtime gating is explicitly satisfied.
templates.remediation_text: Retain preview-only governance until gate conditions and approval posture are formally accepted.
templates.audit_note_text: Structural readiness does not imply or authorize execution.

templates.executive_summary_text: The chain is blocked by one or more governance or structural violations.
templates.technical_summary_text: Blocked reasons prevent readiness progression and must be resolved before the chain can advance.
templates.remediation_text: Address the highest-priority blocked reason first and re-evaluate only after remediation is complete.
templates.audit_note_text: Blocked posture prevented execution-ready classification and no runtime action occurred.

templates.executive_summary_text: The chain is acceptable for governed interpretation but contains a preserved mismatch note.
templates.technical_summary_text: A known sentinel inconsistency remains visible for regression and consistency-review purposes.
templates.remediation_text: Either align the affected fields or keep the mismatch explicitly documented as a preserved validation sentinel.
templates.audit_note_text: Mismatch visibility was preserved intentionally and not normalized away.

templates.executive_summary_text: A terminal governance violation prevents further readiness progression under the current policy posture.
templates.technical_summary_text: Terminal trust, tenant, approval, or rollback conditions require policy or boundary change before retry is appropriate.
templates.remediation_text: Escalate to the relevant governance owner; do not retry until the governing condition changes.
templates.audit_note_text: Terminal governance posture was enforced and no retry was recommended.

qa_checks.criteria: Severity labels and descriptions match the approved taxonomy.
qa_checks.fail_condition: Severity terms are mixed, inflated, or contradicted.

qa_checks.criteria: Blocked reason wording matches machine-readable tokens and narrative text.
qa_checks.pass_condition: Human summaries clearly correspond to blocked reason codes.
qa_checks.fail_condition: Blocked reasons are vague, missing, or mislabeled.

qa_checks.criteria: Remediation language is bounded, actionable, and tied to the actual issue class.
qa_checks.pass_condition: Next actions are specific and non-ambiguous.
qa_checks.fail_condition: Remediation language is vague or generic.

qa_checks.criteria: Required and supporting evidence references are present for governed blocked conditions.
qa_checks.fail_condition: Evidence requirements are missing or underspecified.

qa_checks.criteria: Outputs do not imply execution authority where none exists.
qa_checks.pass_condition: Language clearly distinguishes structural readiness from permission.
qa_checks.fail_condition: Text implies the system may execute, approve, or proceed automatically.

qa_checks.criteria: Terms like approved, safe, ready, or allowed are scoped precisely.
qa_checks.pass_condition: Permission language is bounded and field-specific.
qa_checks.fail_condition: Permission language is vague or overstated.

qa_checks.criteria: Tenant rules and violations are described explicitly.
qa_checks.pass_condition: Tenant-compliance posture is understandable to reviewers.
qa_checks.fail_condition: Tenant scope is unclear or under-described.

qa_checks.criteria: Approval presence is not confused with execution authorization.
qa_checks.pass_condition: Approval wording remains bounded and precise.
qa_checks.fail_condition: Approval is described as if it authorizes execution by itself.

qa_checks.criteria: Outputs preserve the active Envoy v0.2.8 security triage boundary.
qa_checks.pass_condition: No text implies release, bridge execution, artifact mutation, or package publish during triage.
qa_checks.fail_condition: Outputs imply release changes or runtime/bridge activity while triage remains active.

legacySource.description: Business-domain MOLT Aim blocks. Aim answers: 'What are we trying to achieve?' Each block describes the desired real-world business outcome — what success looks like when this commercial reasoning path completes.
legacySource.usage: Select the Aim block that best describes the intended business outcome. Aim governs what the response is ultimately trying to accomplish in a commercial context.

REVENUE_GROWTH.description: Aims oriented toward increasing commercial income

blocks.outcome: To close the period at or above the committed revenue number
examples: achieving quarterly ARR target
closing the year at plan
hitting the monthly bookings goal

blocks.outcome: To increase annual recurring revenue through new business and expansion
examples: adding net new ARR above churn
expanding ARR in existing accounts
growing ARR from a new product tier

blocks.outcome: To close larger individual contracts by selling more scope or value
examples: upselling to enterprise tier
adding multi-year prepay
bundling services with software

blocks.outcome: To maintain a healthy volume of qualified opportunities relative to quota
examples: achieving 3x pipeline coverage
ensuring every rep has enough qualified deals
filling early-stage pipeline before end of quarter

blocks.outcome: To generate income from sources that did not previously exist in the business
examples: launching a professional services offering
monetizing a previously free feature
creating a marketplace revenue stream

CUSTOMER_OUTCOMES.description: Aims oriented toward customer satisfaction, retention, and advocacy

blocks.outcome: To keep existing customers renewing and engaged, reducing churn
examples: achieving net revenue retention above 110%
closing 90% of eligible renewals
reducing logo churn below 5% annually

blocks.outcome: To help the customer achieve the outcomes they purchased the product to deliver
examples: getting a customer to their first meaningful ROI
completing implementation within the agreed timeline
achieving the KPIs defined in the success plan

blocks.outcome: To raise NPS, CSAT, or equivalent scores by delivering a better experience
examples: moving NPS from 30 to 50 within 12 months
reducing post-onboarding CSAT complaints
improving support ticket satisfaction scores

blocks.outcome: To convert satisfied customers into active referrers and public champions
examples: generating referenceable customer accounts
securing case study participation
building a panel of reference customers for analyst briefings

blocks.outcome: To shorten the time between purchase and the customer realizing measurable benefit
examples: getting customers live in under 30 days
delivering first ROI in the first quarter
reducing implementation duration by 40%

MARKET_POSITION.description: Aims oriented toward competitive standing and brand strength

blocks.outcome: To increase the percentage of total available spend that flows to the business
examples: growing from 5% to 8% market share in a segment
displacing a competitor in key accounts
capturing share in a new geography

blocks.outcome: To be recognized as the leading player in a defined market category
examples: achieving Gartner Magic Quadrant leader placement
becoming the default choice for a buyer persona
owning the brand association for a problem category

blocks.outcome: To make the offering meaningfully distinct in ways that matter to buyers
examples: demonstrating a capability gap vs. the nearest competitor
winning on governance and auditability against alternatives
building a moat through proprietary data or technology

blocks.outcome: To increase unaided brand awareness among target buyers
examples: raising brand awareness score in ICP segment by 20%
getting the brand mentioned in industry analyst reports
achieving recognition at target conferences

blocks.outcome: To achieve initial traction in a new geographic or vertical market
examples: closing first 10 customers in a new country
landing reference accounts in a new vertical
establishing a beachhead in an adjacent segment

FINANCIAL_HEALTH.description: Aims oriented toward profitability, efficiency, and financial sustainability

blocks.outcome: To reach a state where revenue consistently exceeds costs
examples: reaching EBITDA breakeven
achieving gross margin targets
becoming cash flow positive

blocks.outcome: To lower the cost of acquiring and serving customers relative to the value they generate
examples: reducing CAC by 20%
improving LTV/CAC ratio above 3x
increasing gross margin through cost reduction

blocks.outcome: To increase the time the business can operate before requiring additional capital
examples: reducing burn rate below $500k/month
achieving 18 months of runway post-raise
cutting operating costs to extend to next milestone

blocks.outcome: To deliver the agreed earnings before interest, tax, depreciation, and amortization
examples: meeting the board-approved EBITDA plan
returning to positive EBITDA after an investment cycle
improving EBITDA margin by 5 points year on year

blocks.outcome: To deploy available funds in the combination of activities that produces the highest return
examples: shifting spend from low-ROI channels to high performers
prioritizing product investment over headcount growth
retiring inefficient programs to fund a strategic bet

OPERATIONAL_EXCELLENCE.description: Aims oriented toward efficiency, quality, and reliable delivery

blocks.outcome: To deliver the same or better results with fewer resources or less time
examples: reducing cost per unit served
automating a manual process to free up headcount
cutting meeting overhead without losing coordination

blocks.outcome: To meet all contractual service level commitments to customers
examples: resolving 95% of P1 issues within SLA
meeting uptime SLA across all enterprise customers
responding to support tickets within committed time windows

blocks.outcome: To lower the rate of mistakes, rework, and defects in business processes
examples: reducing billing errors to under 0.1%
cutting data entry errors in order processing
improving compliance with the expense policy

blocks.outcome: To grow delivery capacity without increasing costs at the same rate
examples: doubling customer count without doubling support team
tripling transaction volume with the same infrastructure team
growing revenue per employee year on year

blocks.outcome: To complete commitments within agreed timeframes consistently
examples: shipping the product milestone on the committed date
completing client deliverables before contractual deadline
hitting the quarterly close on schedule

PEOPLE_TALENT.description: Aims oriented toward building, retaining, and developing a high-performing workforce

blocks.outcome: To fill all approved headcount with qualified candidates within the target timeframe
examples: closing all Q1 engineering headcount by end of quarter
filling a critical VP role within 60 days
hitting a headcount plan for a new office opening

blocks.outcome: To keep high-performing employees engaged and prevent attrition
examples: reducing top-quartile attrition below 5%
retaining all critical sales performers through a reset
maintaining key technical talent through a product pivot

blocks.outcome: To raise commitment, motivation, and satisfaction scores across the workforce
examples: improving engagement survey score by 10 points
reducing voluntary turnover below 12%
achieving eNPS above 30

blocks.outcome: To develop internal candidates who are ready to step into senior roles
examples: identifying and developing two internal VP candidates
ensuring every director has a successor identified
completing a high-potential leadership program

blocks.outcome: To give the organization the specific capability it lacks and needs to execute
examples: building an in-house AI engineering team
developing a sales team's enterprise discovery skills
training the ops team on a new ERP system

STRATEGIC_POSITIONING.description: Aims oriented toward long-term competitive advantage and strategic clarity

blocks.outcome: To align the entire organization on a clear, shared direction and set of priorities
examples: completing an annual strategy refresh with leadership alignment
communicating a pivot clearly to all employees
closing the gap between stated strategy and resource allocation

blocks.outcome: To create structural advantages that are difficult for competitors to replicate
examples: building a proprietary data asset
achieving network effects in a marketplace
establishing a dominant partner ecosystem

blocks.outcome: To raise capital that enables execution of a defined growth strategy
examples: closing a Series B to fund enterprise GTM build-out
securing a strategic investor with sector expertise
raising a bridge to reach a profitability milestone

blocks.outcome: To acquire a company or asset that accelerates strategic goals
examples: acquiring a competitor to consolidate market share
buying a team to close a critical technology gap
acquiring a customer base through a tuck-in acquisition

blocks.outcome: To reach a state where the business can be sold, listed, or transitioned successfully
examples: preparing clean financials and a clean cap table for an M&A process
achieving the ARR and growth rate for an IPO filing
completing a management buyout preparation

COMPLIANCE_RISK.description: Aims oriented toward meeting obligations and protecting the business from risk

blocks.outcome: To demonstrate full compliance to a regulator or certification body
examples: passing a GDPR audit with no findings
achieving SOC 2 Type II certification
clearing an SEC examination

blocks.outcome: To eliminate a known deviation from required legal or regulatory standards
examples: remediating a data handling gap flagged in an audit
updating a contract template to reflect new regulation
closing a HIPAA compliance finding

blocks.outcome: To minimize the financial and reputational risk from litigation or regulatory action
examples: resolving a contractual dispute before litigation
limiting liability through indemnity restructuring
proactively addressing a regulatory gray area

blocks.outcome: To secure legal ownership of proprietary assets and prevent unauthorized use
examples: filing patents before a public announcement
registering trademarks in key markets
enforcing IP rights against an infringer

blocks.outcome: To uphold board-level and regulatory governance requirements consistently
examples: maintaining an independent board with required committee structure
filing required corporate disclosures on time
documenting and following a board-approved policy framework

PARTNERSHIP_OUTCOMES.description: Aims oriented toward creating and extracting value from partnerships

blocks.outcome: To generate a defined volume of qualified opportunities via the partner channel
examples: achieving 20% of new pipeline from partners
activating a set of resellers to hit a sourced pipeline target
launching a referral program that generates 50 leads per quarter

blocks.outcome: To close a higher proportion of deals when working jointly with a partner
examples: improving partner-assisted win rate above 60%
closing a strategic deal with a SI partner
winning a competitive deal through cloud marketplace co-sell

blocks.outcome: To create a network of engaged partners who regularly generate business value
examples: activating 50 selling partners in 12 months
building a community of certified implementation partners
establishing a marketplace with active ISV participants

blocks.outcome: To establish a formalized partnership with a major player that creates structural advantage
examples: signing a strategic go-to-market agreement with a cloud provider
formalizing a technology integration partnership with a market leader
establishing a co-development agreement with a strategic vendor

blocks.aim: Reduce cost of sales via partners
blocks.outcome: To lower the average cost to acquire a customer by shifting volume to partner channels
examples: shifting 30% of deals to lower-cost partner channel
reducing direct sales headcount through channel leverage
achieving partner-assisted deals at 40% lower CAC

PRODUCT_SUCCESS.description: Aims oriented toward building and delivering products that achieve market traction

blocks.outcome: To reach a state where the product demonstrably meets the needs of a repeatable customer segment
examples: achieving 40%+ would-be-very-disappointed score on a Superhuman-style PMF survey
reaching a segment where organic word of mouth is growing
demonstrating consistent renewal and expansion in a defined ICP

blocks.outcome: To deliver committed product milestones within the planned timeframe and scope
examples: shipping a promised enterprise feature before a renewal deadline
delivering a roadmap quarter on time and within scope
meeting a committed launch date

blocks.outcome: To increase active usage of the product among the target user base
examples: growing daily active users by 30%
increasing feature adoption in the customer base
improving activation rate from signup to first key action

blocks.outcome: To prevent customers from leaving because of product gaps, bugs, or poor experience
examples: eliminating the most common product-cited churn reasons
reducing support escalations caused by product issues
improving stability and reducing critical bugs in production

blocks.aim: Win a competitive deal on product
blocks.outcome: To beat an alternative in a direct comparison on product capability, fit, or innovation
examples: winning an enterprise deal where the competitor had more features
demonstrating a differentiating capability in a bake-off
winning a POC evaluation against a named alternative

BRAND_REPUTATION.description: Aims oriented toward earning and protecting a strong market reputation

blocks.outcome: To shift how target buyers think and feel about the brand positively
examples: moving from unknown to recognized in a new segment
rebuilding trust after a public incident
improving analyst perception in a Gartner report

blocks.outcome: To secure favorable coverage in outlets and channels the target audience trusts
examples: landing a product launch story in a tier-one trade publication
generating analyst coverage for a new category
getting a founder feature in a business publication

blocks.outcome: To be recognized as a credible expert voice on a topic that matters to buyers
examples: publishing an original research report that earns media coverage
building a speaker presence at industry conferences
developing a podcast that becomes the reference in the category

blocks.outcome: To limit reputational damage and restore confidence during a public incident
examples: managing communications around a data breach
responding effectively to a negative viral moment
restoring customer trust after a major service outage

blocks.outcome: To attract top candidates by being known as a great place to work
examples: improving Glassdoor rating above 4.2
being recognized on a best-places-to-work list
generating qualified inbound job applications from brand pull

SCALE_EFFICIENCY.description: Aims oriented toward growing the business without growing costs proportionally

blocks.outcome: To replace manual effort with reliable automation in a repeatable business operation
examples: automating invoice generation and delivery
automating a first-line support triage
automating a routine compliance check

blocks.outcome: To grow revenue faster than headcount, improving organizational productivity
examples: growing revenue per FTE from $300k to $400k
scaling ARR 2x with the same commercial team
delivering more output per employee through tooling investment

blocks.outcome: To lower the cost of customer support without reducing satisfaction
examples: deflecting 40% of tickets through self-service content
reducing average handle time with AI-assisted support
shifting volume from high-cost channels to lower-cost digital

blocks.outcome: To create a consistent, documented process that new people can follow reliably
examples: standardizing the enterprise sales discovery process
creating a repeatable customer onboarding playbook
building a standard vendor evaluation framework

blocks.outcome: To shorten the average duration from job opening to accepted offer
examples: reducing time-to-hire from 60 to 30 days
standardizing interview process to remove bottlenecks
implementing sourcing automation to reduce sourcing time

TRANSFORMATION_OUTCOMES.description: Aims oriented toward organizational and business model transformation

blocks.outcome: To modernize core business processes and systems to operate effectively in a digital environment
examples: migrating from legacy ERP to cloud-native platform
moving from manual sales process to CRM-driven workflow
transitioning from paper-based to digital-first operations

blocks.outcome: To evolve from selling a single product to operating an extensible platform with ecosystem value
examples: building an API and marketplace around a core product
enabling third-party developers to build on the platform
generating ecosystem revenue alongside direct product revenue

blocks.outcome: To bring a newly acquired company into operations without disrupting either entity
examples: integrating systems and data within 12 months post-close
unifying GTM teams after a merger
consolidating support and customer success operations

blocks.outcome: To change the fundamental way the business creates and captures value
examples: transitioning from perpetual license to SaaS subscription
shifting from services-led to product-led growth
moving from a direct sales model to a PLG motion

blocks.outcome: To re-stabilize a team and restore high performance after a reorganization
examples: achieving full team productivity within 60 days of a reorg
aligning a newly merged team on shared goals and culture
restoring engagement scores after a workforce reduction

INVESTOR_STAKEHOLDER.description: Aims oriented toward satisfying investors, board, and key stakeholders

blocks.outcome: To complete a capital raise and receive funds from investors
examples: closing a Series A within 6 months of launch
completing a bridge round to extend runway
closing an oversubscribed round from strategic investors

blocks.outcome: To deliver the performance and governance standards the board has approved
examples: hitting the plan approved at the last board meeting
delivering a clean audit with no material findings
maintaining the governance commitments in the investor agreement

blocks.outcome: To generate financial returns for shareholders through growth or liquidity events
examples: achieving a valuation step-up at next round
delivering an M&A exit at target multiple
generating distributions to investors from operating profit

blocks.outcome: To build the business to the scale and metrics that justify a defined valuation
examples: growing to the ARR and growth rate that supports a Series C valuation
achieving the metrics required for an IPO at target price
meeting the acquisition price expectations of a strategic buyer

blocks.outcome: To keep existing investors informed and supportive through good and difficult periods
examples: communicating a missed quarter with credibility and a clear plan
maintaining constructive board relationships through a pivot
building investor trust through consistent reporting and follow-through

GTM_EXECUTION.description: Aims oriented toward effective go-to-market execution

blocks.outcome: To bring a new product to market in a way that generates immediate traction
examples: achieving target sign-ups in the first 30 days post-launch
generating press coverage and qualified leads from a launch event
landing first 10 enterprise customers in the launch quarter

blocks.outcome: To develop a predictable, documentable process that reliably converts qualified leads to customers
examples: codifying the enterprise sales playbook so all reps follow it
achieving predictable win rates from a defined sales stage process
reducing deal cycle variance through a standardized motion

blocks.outcome: To close a higher proportion of contested opportunities
examples: improving overall win rate from 25% to 35%
increasing win rate against the named competitor
improving technical evaluation win rate through better POC management

blocks.outcome: To shorten the time from initial opportunity to closed deal
examples: cutting average cycle from 90 to 60 days
accelerating SMB deals to under 30 days
removing a procurement bottleneck that was adding 3 weeks

blocks.outcome: To ensure every target account in the addressable market is being actively pursued
examples: assigning an SDR or AE to every ICP account in the territory
ensuring all named accounts are contacted at least once per quarter
achieving full coverage of a target industry vertical

LEARNING_IMPROVEMENT.description: Aims oriented toward improving the business through systematic learning

blocks.outcome: To understand why deals were won or lost and use that to improve future performance
examples: interviewing buyers in 10 recent losses to identify common themes
conducting a competitive win analysis to document differentiators
using win/loss data to revise the sales playbook

blocks.outcome: To assess whether a project or change delivered its intended outcome and what can be learned
examples: reviewing a CRM implementation against original success criteria
evaluating a marketing campaign against its objectives
reviewing a reorg against its stated goals 6 months later

blocks.outcome: To ensure the team has the information and skills needed to perform at a high level
examples: assessing sales skills gaps after a quota miss
identifying product knowledge gaps in the support team
finding and closing competitive intelligence gaps in marketing

blocks.outcome: To capture and systematize what the organization has learned so it persists beyond individuals
examples: documenting the sales playbook after a successful quarter
creating a customer success handbook from experienced CS reps
building a knowledge base from top-performing support resolutions

blocks.outcome: To make better choices by using more rigorous frameworks, better data, and clearer criteria
examples: implementing a structured product prioritization framework
using data to replace gut feel in hiring decisions
adopting a formal business case process for investment decisions

CULTURE_WELLBEING.description: Aims oriented toward organizational health and employee wellbeing

blocks.outcome: To create an environment where people consistently do great work and hold each other to high standards
examples: establishing a culture of direct feedback and accountability
building a high-transparency operating cadence
creating a values-aligned recognition program

blocks.outcome: To create conditions where people feel safe to speak up, take risks, and admit mistakes
examples: training managers on creating safe team environments
establishing a blameless post-mortem culture
reducing fear of speaking up in leadership meetings

blocks.outcome: To lower unsustainable workload and stress before it causes attrition and health problems
examples: implementing a sustainable meeting culture
setting clear work-hours expectations
identifying and addressing the root causes of team overload

blocks.outcome: To raise the standard of people management across the organization through training and accountability
examples: training all managers on performance conversations
establishing a manager effectiveness feedback process
identifying and developing the bottom quartile of managers

blocks.outcome: To build a workplace where people of all backgrounds can contribute fully and feel they belong
examples: achieving gender parity at director level and above
building inclusion into hiring and promotion decisions
reducing attrition gaps between demographic groups

DATA_INTELLIGENCE.description: Aims oriented toward using data to make the business smarter and faster

blocks.outcome: To embed data-informed decision making as the default across the organization
examples: moving from gut-feel to metric-based performance conversations
getting every team to report on KPIs weekly
making self-serve analytics available to all business leaders

blocks.aim: Achieve a single source of truth
blocks.outcome: To eliminate conflicting data and align the organization on one authoritative set of numbers
examples: resolving the conflict between finance and sales revenue figures
building a unified customer data platform
standardizing the definition of ARR across the company

blocks.outcome: To forecast customer actions — churn, expansion, support volume — with enough accuracy to act on
examples: building a churn prediction model with 80% accuracy
forecasting renewal outcomes 90 days in advance
predicting expansion opportunity with enough lead time to act

blocks.outcome: To reduce the variance between predicted and actual business outcomes
examples: improving sales forecast accuracy to within 5% of plan
reducing financial forecast miss rate
improving demand planning accuracy for operations

blocks.outcome: To determine through data analysis which investments will generate the greatest return
examples: identifying the product feature most correlated with retention
finding the marketing channel with the lowest CAC
identifying the customer segment with the highest LTV/CAC

SUSTAINABILITY_IMPACT.description: Aims oriented toward responsible business and long-term societal impact

blocks.outcome: To meet defined environmental commitments such as carbon, waste, or energy goals
examples: reaching carbon neutrality by a committed date
reducing operational energy consumption by 30%
eliminating single-use plastics from operations

blocks.outcome: To produce credible, complete disclosures that meet stakeholder expectations
examples: publishing an annual ESG report to GRI standards
completing a TCFD climate disclosure
achieving a target ESG rating from an external agency

blocks.outcome: To create measurable benefit for communities, employees, or customers beyond commercial gain
examples: launching a community investment program
committing 1% of ARR to social initiatives
achieving living wage certification for all employees

blocks.outcome: To embed responsible AI principles into how AI systems are built, governed, and deployed
examples: adopting a formal AI ethics policy
deploying AI with auditable governance via UMG
establishing an AI review board for high-stakes applications

blocks.outcome: To ensure the full supplier ecosystem meets ethical and environmental standards
examples: auditing tier-1 suppliers for labor practices
requiring environmental standards from key vendors
removing suppliers who fail ethical assessments

UMG_BUSINESS_AIMS.description: Aims specific to deploying UMG in commercial and organizational contexts

blocks.aim: Deploy a governed AI commercial agent
blocks.outcome: To put an auditable, policy-compliant AI agent into a business function that produces measurable results
examples: activating a governed AI SDR that books qualified meetings
deploying a governed support agent that resolves tickets within policy
launching a governed BI agent that delivers accurate executive reports

blocks.outcome: To ensure AI decisions in regulated business contexts are fully traceable and defensible
examples: producing a Decision Trace for every AI-assisted credit decision
generating auditable AI outputs for a financial services workflow
meeting a regulator's AI transparency requirements with UMG traces

blocks.outcome: To prevent AI agents from taking unauthorized commercial actions through governance
examples: blocking an AI from offering unauthorized discounts
preventing an AI from making contractual commitments outside scope
ensuring AI communications stay within legal and brand guidelines

blocks.aim: Scale AI-assisted operations without proportional risk
blocks.outcome: To grow the use of AI in business operations while keeping governance tight
examples: tripling AI-assisted transactions without relaxing compliance controls
expanding AI agent deployment across geographies under unified governance
growing AI coverage without increasing compliance overhead proportionally

blocks.outcome: To provide verifiable evidence of responsible AI deployment to customers, regulators, and investors
examples: publishing a governance report for enterprise customer due diligence
providing audit evidence to a regulator asking about AI use
presenting AI governance architecture to a board risk committee

title: aim.coding.library.v1.0.0
reason: unsupported role aim preserved in legacy only

legacySource.description: Coding-domain MOLT Aim blocks. Aim answers: 'What are we trying to achieve?' Each block describes the desired real-world software engineering outcome — what success looks like when this coding reasoning path completes.
legacySource.usage: Select the Aim block that best describes the intended engineering outcome. Aim governs what the response is ultimately trying to accomplish in a software development context.

CORRECTNESS.description: Aims oriented toward making code do the right thing

blocks.outcome: To eliminate a specific defect so the code behaves as intended
examples: patching the function that returns the wrong value
resolving a race condition in concurrent code
correcting the logic error in a conditional branch

blocks.outcome: To bring the test suite to a fully green state with no failures or skips
examples: fixing failing unit tests after a refactor
making an integration test pass against a real service
resolving flaky tests so CI is reliable

blocks.outcome: To ensure the code behaves correctly for boundary inputs, empty inputs, and unexpected values
examples: handling a null input without crashing
correctly processing an empty list
handling integer overflow without silent truncation

blocks.outcome: To produce code that matches every stated requirement in the specification
examples: implementing every field in an OpenAPI response schema
building every acceptance criterion in a user story
matching every constraint in a formal protocol spec

blocks.outcome: To ensure the code produces the same result for the same inputs every time
examples: removing non-deterministic map iteration from an output
fixing a test that passes or fails depending on timing
eliminating a dependency on unordered set output

CODE_QUALITY.description: Aims oriented toward producing clean, maintainable code

blocks.outcome: To make code easier for the next developer to understand without documentation
examples: renaming variables to express intent clearly
extracting a complex expression into a named function
replacing a magic number with a named constant

blocks.outcome: To eliminate repeated logic by expressing it once in a reusable form
examples: extracting a shared validation into a helper
replacing copy-pasted code with a parameterised function
centralising a repeated database query pattern

blocks.outcome: To reduce the cognitive load required to understand or modify a function or module
examples: decomposing a 200-line function into smaller ones
flattening deeply nested conditional logic
reducing cyclomatic complexity below the team threshold

blocks.outcome: To produce code that satisfies all automated style, type, and quality gates
examples: resolving all ESLint errors and warnings
fixing all mypy type errors
passing a strict TypeScript compile with no errors

blocks.outcome: To produce code that passes peer review without requiring significant changes
examples: writing code that reviewers approve on first pass
matching the team's established patterns and conventions
producing a PR that generates no blocking comments

PERFORMANCE.description: Aims oriented toward making code faster or more efficient

blocks.outcome: To ensure requests or operations complete within the required time
examples: reducing a slow endpoint below 200ms p99
making a background job complete within its time window
bringing a render time under the user-perceivable threshold

blocks.outcome: To lower the amount of RAM the program consumes during execution
examples: fixing a memory leak in a long-running service
reducing the heap footprint of a large data structure
streaming data instead of loading it all into memory

blocks.outcome: To increase the volume of work the system can process per unit of time
examples: doubling the number of requests per second a service handles
processing a data pipeline twice as fast
increasing message consumption rate for a queue consumer

blocks.outcome: To lower the processor time consumed by the program
examples: replacing an expensive algorithm with a more efficient one
reducing redundant computation in a hot loop
caching a result that was being recalculated on every call

blocks.outcome: To make database operations faster by improving query design or indexes
examples: reducing a slow query from 2s to 20ms with an index
eliminating an N+1 with a join
rewriting a correlated subquery as a lateral join

SECURITY.description: Aims oriented toward making code safe from attack and misuse

blocks.outcome: To remove a specific security flaw that could be exploited
examples: patching an SQL injection vector
fixing a reflected XSS in a template
removing a path traversal in a file handler

blocks.aim: Achieve zero critical CVEs in dependencies
blocks.outcome: To have no known critical vulnerabilities in any dependency at time of deployment
examples: upgrading all packages with critical CVEs
replacing a vulnerable library with a safe alternative
pinning a patched version of a transitive dependency

blocks.outcome: To ensure code and systems request and use only the minimum permissions needed
examples: scoping an API token to read-only
removing an overly broad IAM role
limiting a service account to the resources it actually uses

blocks.outcome: To satisfy a formal review of code or system security with no critical findings
examples: clearing all critical and high findings from a penetration test
resolving all issues flagged by a SAST tool
passing an external code security review

blocks.outcome: To ensure credentials, keys, and tokens never appear in code, logs, or responses
examples: removing a hardcoded API key from source code
preventing a token from appearing in an error response
scanning commit history and rotating any exposed secrets

RELIABILITY.description: Aims oriented toward keeping code running correctly under adverse conditions

blocks.outcome: To maintain service availability at or above the committed SLA
examples: reaching 99.9% uptime for a critical service
eliminating a class of crashes that was causing downtime
making a service resilient to a dependency failure

blocks.outcome: To prevent a service from failing when request volume or data size increases
examples: fixing an out-of-memory crash at high concurrency
resolving a stack overflow on large inputs
preventing a connection pool exhaustion under load

blocks.outcome: To ensure the system continues to provide partial functionality when a dependency is unavailable
examples: returning a cached result when the database is slow
disabling a non-critical feature when an external API is down
falling back to a default when a config service is unavailable

blocks.outcome: To ensure that repeating an operation produces the same result as running it once
examples: making a payment handler idempotent with a deduplication key
ensuring a migration can be run twice without harm
making a message consumer safe to retry

blocks.outcome: To minimise the time to restore normal operation after a failure
examples: implementing automatic restart on crash
adding a circuit breaker to cut off a failing dependency
enabling zero-downtime deploys to remove recovery time on release

TESTABILITY.description: Aims oriented toward making code easy to verify

blocks.outcome: To ensure a specified percentage of code is exercised by tests
examples: reaching 80% line coverage on a module
achieving full branch coverage on a critical path
covering every public API method with at least one test

blocks.outcome: To restructure code so that its units can be tested in isolation without external dependencies
examples: introducing dependency injection to allow mocking
separating I/O from pure logic
extracting a side-effect-free function from a coupled block

blocks.outcome: To make every test produce a consistent result regardless of timing, order, or environment
examples: fixing a test that depends on wall clock time
removing a test that relies on network access
resolving a test that fails when run in a different order

blocks.outcome: To ensure developers can run tests quickly enough not to avoid them
examples: bringing a full test run under 60 seconds
parallelising a slow test suite
mocking slow I/O to make unit tests near-instant

blocks.outcome: To demonstrate a bug or new requirement with a test before writing the fix or feature
examples: writing a test that reproduces a reported bug
writing a test for an acceptance criterion before implementation
producing a minimal failing example that proves the issue

MAINTAINABILITY.description: Aims oriented toward making code easy to change over time

blocks.outcome: To lower the degree to which components depend on the internal details of each other
examples: introducing an interface between two tightly coupled modules
removing a direct import of an implementation detail
replacing a shared global with a passed dependency

blocks.outcome: To ensure each module or function has a single, well-defined responsibility
examples: splitting a class that does both data access and business logic
separating formatting from computation in a function
grouping related functions into a dedicated module

blocks.outcome: To ensure that modifying one part of the codebase does not unexpectedly break another
examples: adding tests to cover a module before refactoring it
introducing a typed interface before changing an implementation
running a full regression suite after a structural change

blocks.aim: Reduce time to onboard a new developer
blocks.outcome: To make the codebase understandable quickly to someone who is new to it
examples: improving naming so the purpose of each module is clear
documenting the setup steps in a README
adding architecture diagrams that show component relationships

blocks.outcome: To remove workarounds, outdated patterns, and known problems from the codebase
examples: replacing a deprecated library with a current alternative
removing a TODO that has sat unaddressed for months
deleting dead code that was never removed

SCALABILITY.description: Aims oriented toward making systems handle more load or data

blocks.outcome: To architect and implement changes that allow the system to handle an order of magnitude more requests
examples: moving from a single-instance to a horizontally scaled service
introducing a cache layer before a bottleneck database
migrating to a distributed queue for processing spikes

blocks.outcome: To split a single overloaded resource across multiple units to distribute load
examples: sharding a high-write table across multiple databases
partitioning a Kafka topic for parallel consumption
splitting a large in-memory cache across Redis nodes

blocks.aim: Remove a single point of failure
blocks.outcome: To eliminate any component whose failure would bring down the entire system
examples: adding a replica to a single-instance database
deploying an application across multiple availability zones
replacing a single queue broker with a replicated cluster

blocks.outcome: To make the system capable of automatically adding or removing resources based on demand
examples: making application instances stateless so they can scale horizontally
configuring a Kubernetes HPA on a CPU metric
designing a Lambda-based handler to scale to zero and back

blocks.outcome: To lower the rate at which concurrent writes block each other
examples: using optimistic locking instead of pessimistic locks
batching writes to reduce transaction frequency
introducing an append-only log to avoid row-level lock contention

DELIVERY.description: Aims oriented toward shipping code to users safely and quickly

blocks.outcome: To deliver working code to real users through the full release process
examples: merging a feature PR, passing CI, and deploying to prod
completing a feature flag rollout to 100% of users
delivering a hotfix to production within an SLA

blocks.outcome: To release a new version without any interruption to users
examples: using a rolling deploy with health checks
implementing blue-green deployment
using a feature flag to decouple deploy from release

blocks.outcome: To shorten the time from code merge to production availability
examples: parallelising CI pipeline steps
caching build dependencies
optimising a Docker build with layer caching

blocks.outcome: To ensure that a problematic release can be quickly reversed to a known-good state
examples: keeping the previous image tag in registry before a deploy
using a feature flag to disable a new code path instantly
maintaining a database migration that can be reversed

blocks.outcome: To satisfy every required check before code is allowed to deploy to production
examples: passing all CI tests before merge
clearing a security scan before release
satisfying a required code review approval

OBSERVABILITY.description: Aims oriented toward understanding system behavior in production

blocks.outcome: To ensure that when something goes wrong in production, it can be diagnosed quickly
examples: adding structured logs to every error path
instrumenting requests with trace IDs
ensuring every exception reaches the error tracker with full context

blocks.outcome: To follow any request from entry to completion across all services it touches
examples: propagating trace context across service boundaries
correlating logs with a shared trace ID
visualising the full span tree of a slow request

blocks.outcome: To configure alerting that fires on genuine user-impacting conditions without false positives
examples: alerting on p99 latency rather than average
alerting on error rate rather than total error count
reducing alert noise so on-call engineers trust alerts

blocks.outcome: To build visualisations that let engineers understand system health at a glance
examples: building a golden signals dashboard for a service
visualising queue depth alongside consumer lag
showing request volume, error rate, and latency side by side

blocks.outcome: To ensure a service consistently performs within its service level objective
examples: keeping error rate below 0.1% of requests
maintaining p99 latency under 500ms for 30 days
achieving 99.95% availability over a rolling month

ARCHITECTURE.description: Aims oriented toward achieving sound structural design

blocks.outcome: To ensure each architectural layer only depends on the layers it is permitted to know about
examples: preventing a controller from containing business logic
stopping a domain model from importing from a persistence layer
ensuring an API handler does not call a third-party library directly

blocks.outcome: To move a piece of logic out of an application into a shared package usable by multiple consumers
examples: extracting a shared utility into an internal library
packaging a UMG block serialiser as a standalone module
moving shared validation logic into a published package

blocks.outcome: To extract a self-contained domain from a monolith into an independently deployable service
examples: extracting user management into a separate service
separating billing from the core application
moving notification logic into a dedicated service

blocks.outcome: To handle a recurring concern — logging, auth, rate limiting — consistently across all services
examples: centralising auth enforcement in a gateway
standardising logging format across all services
moving rate limiting out of individual services into infrastructure

blocks.outcome: To evolve an interface or data format without breaking existing consumers
examples: adding a new field without removing old ones
releasing a new API version while keeping v1 working
maintaining a data migration that supports both old and new formats

DEVELOPER_EXPERIENCE.description: Aims oriented toward making the development process faster and less painful

blocks.outcome: To enable a new developer to run the project locally in minutes rather than hours
examples: automating dev environment setup with a script
using Docker Compose to remove manual service setup
providing a one-command dev bootstrap

blocks.outcome: To reduce the time between making a change and seeing its effect
examples: enabling hot module replacement in the dev server
making unit tests run in under 5 seconds
adding a watch mode to the build tool

blocks.outcome: To make error output clear enough that developers understand what went wrong and how to fix it
examples: adding context to a generic exception message
improving a compiler error to suggest the fix
making a validation error name the field and the constraint that failed

blocks.outcome: To ensure all developers use the same editors, linters, formatters, and build tools
examples: committing an .editorconfig to the repo
enforcing formatter settings in CI
providing a recommended VS Code extensions file

blocks.outcome: To replace a task developers repeat constantly with a reliable automated equivalent
examples: scripting a release process that was done manually
automating test data seeding
generating a changelog entry from conventional commits

MIGRATION.description: Aims oriented toward safely moving code, data, or infrastructure from one state to another

blocks.outcome: To evolve the database schema without data loss or production downtime
examples: adding a column to a large table without locking
renaming a column across multiple deploys
backfilling a new field from existing data safely

blocks.outcome: To replace an outdated library with a current alternative without breaking behavior
examples: migrating from Moment.js to date-fns
replacing a deprecated ORM with a current version
switching from an EOL runtime version to a supported one

blocks.aim: Upgrade a language or framework version
blocks.outcome: To move the project to a newer runtime or framework version successfully
examples: migrating from Python 3.9 to 3.12
upgrading from React 17 to React 19
migrating from Rails 6 to Rails 7

blocks.outcome: To move all consumers from an old API version to a new one and decommission the old

blocks.outcome: To replace manually managed infrastructure with version-controlled declarative definitions
examples: converting manually provisioned AWS resources to Terraform
migrating Kubernetes manifests into Helm charts
replacing a hand-configured server with an Ansible playbook

INTEGRATION.description: Aims oriented toward connecting systems or components correctly

blocks.outcome: To connect the application to an external service so data flows correctly between them
examples: integrating a payment gateway
connecting to a mapping API
adding OAuth2 login via an identity provider

blocks.outcome: To expose an endpoint that correctly receives, validates, and processes events from an external system
examples: building a Stripe webhook handler

processing Slack event callbacks correctly

blocks.outcome: To ensure messages between producers and consumers are delivered exactly once or at least once with correct handling
examples: implementing idempotent consumer logic
configuring dead letter queue handling
ensuring messages are not lost during a consumer restart

blocks.outcome: To ingest data from a previously untapped source into the system
examples: connecting a Postgres replica for analytics
integrating a third-party data feed via API
ingesting events from a Kafka topic into a data warehouse

blocks.outcome: To verify that a service fulfils the expectations of its consumers at an API boundary
examples: publishing and passing a Pact consumer-driven contract test
verifying a GraphQL schema against a consumer query
confirming an event payload matches the consumer's expected schema

DISCOVERY.description: Aims oriented toward understanding unfamiliar code or systems

blocks.outcome: To build a working mental model of how a codebase is structured and how its parts relate
examples: mapping the entry points and main flows of a new service
identifying where a domain concept is represented in the code
understanding the dependency graph of a module

blocks.aim: Identify the source of a regression
blocks.outcome: To find the commit or change that introduced a broken behavior
examples: bisecting the git history to find a regression commit
diffing two versions of a function to spot the change
correlating a deployment time with a metric degradation

blocks.outcome: To document what a service depends on and what depends on it
examples: tracing all downstream consumers of an API
mapping all upstream dependencies of a service
identifying which services would be affected by a schema change

blocks.outcome: To identify where time or resources are being consumed and why
examples: using a profiler to find the hot function in a slow endpoint
tracing a slow database query back to its ORM call
identifying which dependency is adding the most latency to a request

blocks.outcome: To trigger the same failure in a development environment to enable safe debugging
examples: reconstructing a failing request from production logs
seeding test data that matches a production state
replicating production environment variables locally to reproduce a config-dependent bug

COMPLIANCE_STANDARDS.description: Aims oriented toward meeting coding and regulatory standards

blocks.outcome: To ensure UI code is usable by people with disabilities as required by accessibility guidelines
examples: passing WCAG 2.1 AA for a web interface

adding ARIA labels to all form controls

blocks.outcome: To ensure all dependencies are used under licences that are compatible with the project's distribution model
examples: replacing a GPL dependency in a proprietary project

blocks.outcome: To have automated compliance checking tools report zero critical findings
examples: clearing all critical findings from a DAST scan
resolving all issues from a SAST pipeline run
passing a dependency scan with no high-severity vulnerabilities

blocks.aim: Meet data privacy requirements in code
blocks.outcome: To implement data handling in code that satisfies privacy law requirements
examples: implementing right-to-erasure in the user data layer

adding consent checks before activating tracking code

blocks.outcome: To produce code that conforms to a mandated standard or style guide
examples: meeting a client's internal coding standards for a delivery
conforming to MISRA C for embedded code
following Google's Java style guide across the codebase

KNOWLEDGE_TRANSFER.description: Aims oriented toward sharing understanding of code effectively

blocks.outcome: To produce clear, accurate documentation for code that currently has none
examples: writing docstrings for every public function in a module
adding a README to an undocumented internal library
documenting a complex algorithm with inline explanation

blocks.outcome: To describe what code does in terms that someone without deep technical knowledge can understand
examples: explaining what an API endpoint does to a product manager

walking a junior developer through a complex algorithm

blocks.outcome: To write a structured proposal explaining how a feature or change will be implemented before coding begins
examples: writing a design doc for a new service
producing an RFC for a breaking API change
writing a technical spec for a database schema change

blocks.outcome: To transfer ownership of a codebase or system to another team with enough context for them to operate it
examples: producing a runbook for a service being handed to a different team
writing an onboarding guide for a module being transferred
documenting all known issues and in-progress work before a team change

blocks.outcome: To produce a clear, blameless account of an incident that captures what happened and what will change
examples: writing an incident report after a production outage
documenting a data loss incident timeline and remediation
producing an RCA after a security incident

INNOVATION.description: Aims oriented toward exploring and introducing new technical approaches

blocks.outcome: To demonstrate that a proposed approach is feasible before committing to full implementation
examples: building a spike to validate a new framework choice
prototyping an algorithm before writing production code
running a proof of concept for a new infrastructure pattern

blocks.outcome: To assess whether a tool, library, or platform is suitable for adoption in the project
examples: benchmarking two database options for a workload
comparing two state management libraries for a frontend

blocks.outcome: To adopt a design pattern or coding practice that improves the codebase going forward
examples: introducing repository pattern for data access
adopting event sourcing for a domain
migrating to a command-query separation pattern

blocks.outcome: To create internal tooling that improves the productivity or quality of the engineering team
examples: building a CLI for a common developer task

building a local development helper that mocks external services

blocks.outcome: To release code, a fix, or documentation to an open source project
examples: submitting a bug fix PR to an upstream library

REFACTORING.description: Aims oriented toward improving structure without changing behavior

blocks.outcome: To create a reusable, well-named layer that hides implementation details from callers
examples: introducing a repository interface over direct database calls
extracting a service class from a fat controller
wrapping a third-party API behind an internal adapter

blocks.outcome: To remove code that is no longer executed or used, reducing confusion and maintenance surface
examples: removing an old feature flag branch that is permanently enabled
deleting commented-out code that is no longer relevant
removing an unused exported function from a module

blocks.outcome: To apply a consistent approach to error propagation and reporting across the codebase
examples: replacing mixed exception styles with a unified error type

blocks.outcome: To rename variables, functions, and classes so that purpose is immediately clear
examples: renaming a function called 'process' to 'validateAndNormaliseAddress'
renaming a boolean flag called 'flag' to 'isEmailVerified'

blocks.outcome: To centralise scattered configuration values so they are defined once and used consistently
examples: moving hardcoded values into a single config file

UMG_CODING_AIMS.description: Aims specific to implementing UMG constructs in code

blocks.outcome: To write a compiler stage — governance, trigger, resolution, synthesis — that produces exactly the right output for all inputs
examples: implementing the governance pass that correctly disables OFF'd blocks

blocks.outcome: To generate a trace that fully documents every decision made during a compilation pass
examples: recording every governance action with the rule that caused it

blocks.aim: Pass the UMG conformance test suite
blocks.outcome: To satisfy every test in the compliance test suite for UMG implementors
examples: passing the determinism test with identical outputs for identical inputs
passing the governance supremacy test that verifies OFF cannot be bypassed
passing the trace completeness test that verifies every decision is recorded

blocks.outcome: To verify that the compiler produces identical output every time for the same sleeve, governance, and priority inputs
examples: writing a property test that runs the compiler twice and asserts output equality
removing any randomness from the resolution algorithm
ensuring that compiler output is stable across environments

blocks.outcome: To successfully connect a UMG compiler to a model or agent runtime so that compiled cognition governs execution
examples: wiring a RuntimeSpec into a Claude system prompt correctly
validating that governance OFF states are respected by the model
confirming the Decision Trace is generated and stored after each compilation

title: aim.library.v1.0.0
reason: unsupported role aim preserved in legacy only

legacySource.description: Comprehensive library of atomic MOLT Aim blocks. Aim answers: 'What are we trying to achieve?' Each block describes the desired real-world outcome — what success looks like when this reasoning path completes.
legacySource.usage: Select the Aim block that best describes the intended outcome for the current context. Aim sits above Need in the interpretive chain and governs what the response is ultimately trying to accomplish.

automating a report generation pipeline

VERIFICATION_OUTCOMES.description: Aims oriented toward proving, confirming, and certifying

blocks.outcome: To prove that a system or output does exactly what it claims to do
examples: verifying a RuntimeSpec matches the compiled CIR
confirming a function returns expected output
proving a policy has no gaps

blocks.outcome: To verify that operation poses no unacceptable risk to users or systems
examples: pre-release safety review
robot sleeve force cap verification
governance block OFF state certification

blocks.outcome: To demonstrate that all required elements are present and accounted for
examples: confirming all MOLT types are populated in a sleeve
verifying all canon invariants are covered
checking all acceptance criteria are met

blocks.outcome: To confirm that results are consistent and identical across repeated runs
examples: verifying deterministic compilation
confirming a test produces the same result on re-run
establishing that identical inputs yield identical outputs

blocks.outcome: To resolve every identified defect or gap before delivery
examples: clearing all open ODRs before canon lock
resolving all failing tests before release
addressing all reviewer comments before publication

REFLECTION_OUTCOMES.description: Aims oriented toward learning from experience and improving

blocks.outcome: To surface what worked, what did not, and why after a cycle or project
examples: retrospective after a canon revision
post-incident review
Envoy performance debrief

blocks.outcome: To make the next cycle more effective based on this one
examples: refining the MOLT compilation pipeline
improving block authoring workflow
streamlining the governance review cycle

blocks.outcome: To refine decision-making by comparing predictions to actual outcomes
examples: reviewing whether a priority choice produced the expected synthesis
comparing estimated and actual effort
checking whether a governance rule caught what it was supposed to

blocks.outcome: To explicitly recognize where something went wrong without deflection
examples: naming a mistyped block as the cause of a compilation failure
acknowledging an incorrect audit result
owning a design flaw openly

blocks.outcome: To capture knowledge in a form that persists beyond the people who currently hold it
examples: documenting the UMG genesis snapshot
writing a transfer document for an AI instance
publishing canon decisions with full rationale

UMG_FRAMEWORK_AIMS.description: Aims specific to achieving correct, governed, auditable UMG operation

blocks.outcome: To ensure governance blocks constrain all cognition before any execution proceeds
examples: completing the governance pass before triggers
verifying no block bypasses the governance layer
confirming governance is evaluated first in every compilation

blocks.outcome: To compile a sleeve into a correct, fully governed execution contract
examples: emitting a RuntimeSpec with no OFF'd blocks active
producing a RuntimeSpec that matches the resolved CIR
delivering a RuntimeSpec a safe executor can consume

blocks.outcome: To ensure identical inputs always produce identical compiled outputs
examples: confirming two compiler runs on the same sleeve produce the same RuntimeSpec
verifying no random or context-sensitive resolution occurs
proving compilation is reproducible across Envoy instances

blocks.outcome: To produce a Decision Trace that covers and explains every compilation decision
examples: recording every governance action in the trace
documenting every winner and loser block
ensuring no silent conflict resolution occurs

blocks.outcome: To improve and extend the UMG framework through explicit, versioned, traceable change
examples: resolving an ODR and updating the canon
incrementing the canon version with a documented rationale
adding a new MOLT type with full invariant and non-goal documentation

block.label: LangChain Bridge Runtime Output
block.text: Return result, structured_output, trace_events, tool_calls, denied_tools, approval_requests, warnings, and errors in UMG RuntimeSpec-compatible form.

block.label: LangChain Tool Schema
block.text: Every exposed tool must declare tool_id, tool_name, description, input_schema, output_schema, permission_level, risk_class, requires_approval, runtime_target, and trace_enabled.
